text: The surgeon sharply dissects the attachments between the colon and the abdominal wall, incising the white line of Toldt. The incision is made laterally to the mesentery of the colon, which appears slightly darker yellow compared to the perinephric fat. The mesentery is gently lifted with a grasper, and the fibroareolar attachments are sharply divided.

The surgeon identifies the plane between the intestinal mesentery and Girauda's fascia, which is relatively avascular and can be developed bluntly. A harmonic scalpel is used to divide attachments, mobilizing the colon and spleen medially. The peanut retractor is carefully maneuvered to avoid puncturing the spleen.

The ureter is identified at the lower pole of the kidney, visibly peristalsing. It is retracted superiorly to avoid damage during dissection. The gonadal vein is seen inserting into the renal vein. Dissection continues posterior to the renal vein, revealing the renal artery located just behind it.

The surgeon removes dense perinephric fat using a combination of harmonic and blunt dissection techniques. Laparoscopic ultrasound is employed to define the tumor boundaries. A Satinsky clamp is applied to the hilum, with the artery clamped while the vein remains open. An additional five millimeter trocar is inserted for access.

The surgeon uses sharp scissors to dissect the tumor, sharply encircling the renal capsule to delineate its boundary. Minimal bleeding is observed due to the clamped renal artery. A five millimeter trocar is used to position an assistant for tumor retraction, enhancing visibility. The surgeon controls both suction and scissors, distinguishing normal renal parenchyma from the tumor, which has a distinctly different texture.

The surgeon has detached the specimen from the kidney, indicating that the tumor has been completely passed. Large CPX needles, partially straightened, are inserted through a twelve millimeter trocar below the tumor bed. Figure of eight sutures are placed carefully to cover the entire tumor bed, aiming to prevent bleeding and urine leaks.

The surgeon places a second figure-of-eight suture to stabilize the kidney, ensuring shallow bites to avoid deep tissue penetration. Laparotides are used to secure the suture and achieve hemostasis. The hilar clamp is released, marking a total warm ischemia time of twenty minutes, with the tumor bed appearing completely dry. The specimen, including the perinephric fat, is placed in a ten millimeter endocatch bag for pathology staging analysis.
